figures.caption: Table 1. Quantitative evaluation on the InterHuman test sets. ± indicates a 95% confidence interval and→ means the closer to ground truth the better. Boldface indicates the best result, while underline refers to the second best.
Figure 3. Qualitative comparison (zoom into see it better) between FreeMotion [12] and UMF. Red circles demonstrate successful cases, while Blue circles show failure cases.

Figure 2. Overview of the Unified Motion Flow (UMF) architecture. The UMF framework consists of three stages. (A) Unified motion VAE: A motion VAE with latent adapters encodes raw motions from heterogeneous datasets (e.g., HumanML3D [13], InterHuman [32]) into a regularized multi-token latent representation (Z). (B) P-Flow motion prior generation: The Pyramid Flow Transformer synthesizes the latent motion prior (Ž) based on noisy latent motion and text conditions. The P-Flow operates hierarchically based on the timestep t ∼ (0, 1): it processes downsampled, low-resolution latents for t < p and switches to original-resolution latents for t > p, mitigating multi-token computational overheads. (C) S-Flow reaction generation: Based on the previously generated latent {Ži, . . . , Ž1}, the context adapter generates the context motion C. Then the Semi-Noise Flow transformer predicts the reaction latent (W̌ ) by jointly modeling context reconstruction and reaction transformation, alleviating the error accumulation from previously generated motion.
